items.myth: Cosine similarity and Euclidean distance always rank neighbors the same way.
items.reality: They agree on direction when vectors share the same L2 norm, but when magnitudes differ cosine ignores scale while L2 does not—rankings can diverge.
items.example: Vectors (1,1) and (2,2) have cosine 1.0 but L2 distance sqrt(2) > 0; (1,1) and (1.1, 0.9) may be closer in L2 but less aligned in cosine.

items.myth: The L2 norm is just the square root of the sum of squares—so squaring never matters.
items.reality: Many objectives use squared L2 (||·||₂²) because it is smooth at zero and simplifies derivatives; conflating the two breaks gradient calculations.
items.example: MSE loss is mean squared error (squared L2 per dimension); its derivative is linear in the residual, unlike the derivative of plain L2 which has a singularity at zero.

items.myth: Any non-negative function of coordinate differences is a valid distance.
items.reality: A distance must satisfy symmetry, identity of indiscernibles and the triangle inequality; ad-hoc formulas can violate these.
items.example: d(u,v) = |u₁ - v₁| without combining all coordinates is not a metric on Rⁿ for n>1 because it ignores differences in other dimensions entirely.
